metadata.source: Rare and useful species detail build with clickable detail windows, sightings, and image credits.

records.habitat: Bogs, cold rocky shorelines, and northern barrens.
records.reason: Boreal species with limited native Michigan populations.
key_features: Low evergreen mat-forming shrub.
Needle-like leaves rolled under at the edges.
Black berries on a creeping, woody plant.
Usually found in cold, acidic northern habitat.
distinguishing_features: Unlike bearberry, black crowberry has narrower needle-like leaves rather than broader spoon-shaped leaves.
Unlike blueberries, the fruit sits on a low evergreen mat, not on a deciduous twiggy shrub.
Habitat is a big clue: open boggy or windswept boreal ground.

records.habitat: Cool mossy conifer woods and delicate northern forest floor habitat.
records.reason: Highly sensitive orchid with vulnerable populations.
records.short_reason: Sensitive orchid of cool northern woods.
key_features: Single showy pink-purple slipper-like flower.
Usually only one leaf from the base.
Small and delicate; easy to overlook.
Appears in cool mossy forest settings.
distinguishing_features: Much smaller and more delicate than pink lady's slipper.
Usually a solitary flower with a distinctive pouch and long side petals.
The single basal leaf is a good clue.

images.caption: Calypso bulbosa var. occidentalis, the Fairy Slipper.
images.creator: Walter Siegmund

images.caption: Calypso bulbosa var. occidentalis, the Fairy Slipper.
images.creator: Walter Siegmund

images.caption: Calypso bulbosa
images.creator: Mount Rainier National Park from Ashford, WA, United States

records.habitat: Rocky northern openings, cool slopes, and shore habitats.
records.reason: Northern plant with limited Michigan presence.
records.short_reason: Northern aster with limited state presence.
key_features: Aster-type flower heads with many narrow rays.
Typically found in northern rocky settings.
Growth form more restrained than many common asters.
distinguishing_features: Use habitat and overall plant form, not flower color alone.
Rare rocky northern habitat is an important clue.
Do not assume every purple-blue aster in bloom is this species.

images.caption: Great Northern Aster (Canadanthus modestus) in Canada
images.creator: Dustin Snider

records.habitat: Cold northern wetlands, seepage areas, and rich boreal wet habitat.
records.reason: Arctic-boreal plant with limited Michigan distribution.
key_features: Yellow buttercup flower.
Kidney-shaped leaves divided into three lobes.
Strong association with cold wet northern habitat.
distinguishing_features: Leaf shape matters: look for divided, kidney-based leaves, not just any buttercup flower.
Wet boreal habitat is a stronger clue than bloom color.
This entry also covers the alternate scientific naming Ranunculus lapponicus.

images.caption: Ranunculus flower close to Longyearbyen / Adventdalen, central Spitsbergen - Svalbard.
images.creator: Bjoertvedt

records.habitat: Moist meadows, fens, and open wetland edges.
records.reason: Rare northern gentian with restricted state distribution.
records.short_reason: Rare gentian of open wet ground.
key_features: Blue gentian flowers.
Very narrow linear leaves along the stem.
Typically found in open moist habitat rather than dry upland woods.
distinguishing_features: Leaf shape is a major clue: long narrow leaves set along the stem.
Flower form differs from fringed gentians and bottle gentians.
Use both leaf shape and wet habitat together.

images.caption: stem and leaves of narrow-leaved gentian
images.creator: Ayotte, Gilles, 1948-

key_features: Arching woodland plant.
Bell-like hanging flowers or berries depending on season.
Typically grows in rich northern forest habitat.
distinguishing_features: Growth form and hanging flowers or fruit help separate it from Solomon's seal.
Look closely at leaf arrangement and flower position.
Rich woodland habitat is expected.

images.caption: Prosartes trachycarpa in the Botanischer Garten, Berlin-Dahlem
images.creator: Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz

images.caption: Prosartes trachycarpa at Botanical Gardens Berlin-Dahlem
images.creator: User:BotBln

key_features: Pale gray-green thistle with dune adaptation.
Usually in open sandy Great Lakes dune habitat.
Large thistle heads on a low to medium-height plant.
distinguishing_features: Habitat is critical: dunes, not random roadsides or fields.
Overall pale woolly look is often more subdued than common aggressive thistles.
Do not assume every thistle on sand is Pitcher's thistle.

records.habitat: Cold seeps, springs, and wet edges.
records.reason: Of concern because of limited or vulnerable Michigan populations.
key_features: Yellow monkey-flower blossoms.
Moist seep, spring, or wet edge habitat.
Opposite leaves and soft green stems.
distinguishing_features: Wet habitat is a major clue.
Flower form is more tubular and open-faced than many unrelated yellow flowers.
Use stem, leaf arrangement, and habitat together.

images.caption: Mimulus guttatus in PAN Botanical Garden in Warsaw
images.creator: Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz

records.habitat: Alvar grasslands, bedrock shores, and open calcareous habitats.
records.reason: Rare native wild populations in Michigan; not ordinary garden chives.
records.short_reason: Native wild chive populations, not yard chives.
key_features: Hollow onion-like leaves.
Purple rounded flower heads.
Strong allium smell when crushed.
lookalikes: Death camas look-alike concern when not in flower
distinguishing_features: This entry is about native wild populations, not common yard chives.
Allium odor is important; plants lacking the onion scent need much more caution.
Habitat and wild setting matter a lot.

images.caption: Allium schoenoprasum (chives), cracked flower bud covered with morning dew
images.creator: Dominicus Johannes Bergsma

records.habitat: Northern woods, clearings, and disturbed openings.
records.reason: Rare in Michigan despite wider northern range.
records.short_reason: Northern everlasting with scarce state records.
key_features: Erect everlasting with narrow leaves.
Clustered flower heads rather than showy petals.
Silvery or softly hairy appearance.
distinguishing_features: Overall plant form matters more than any single flower head.
Watch leaf width, stem habit, and the clustered non-showy heads.
This entry also covers the older naming Gnaphalium sylvaticum.

records.habitat: Old hardwood logs and mature forests.
records.reason: Old-forest associated fungus with localized northern records.
key_features: White branching fruitbody with hanging teeth.
Usually on dead hardwood.
Coral-like structure rather than a simple clump.
distinguishing_features: More branched and coral-like than lion's mane.
Look at overall branching structure and where the teeth hang from.
Host wood and form help separate it from other toothed fungi.

images.caption: Hericium coralloides specimen found in Burlington, Vermont, USA.
images.creator: Daniel Josefchak

images.caption: Image of coral tooth fungi, Hericium coralloides
images.creator: Lazar.zenit
images.license: Creative Commons Zero, Public Domain Dedication

records.habitat: Conifer forest with specialized soil and fungal communities.
records.reason: Associated with older conifer systems and not something people report every day in the U.P.
records.short_reason: Distinctive tooth fungus tied to intact conifer habitat.
key_features: Young caps often bead red droplets.
Toothed underside rather than gills or pores.
Usually in conifer settings.
distinguishing_features: The red droplets on young specimens are a strong clue but not always present.
Check the toothed underside.
Older specimens can look less dramatic, so structure matters.

records.reason: Often treated as uncommon to rare in parts of North America and worth tracking carefully.
key_features: Purple to lilac-brown ear or vase-shaped fruitbodies.
Wrinkled or ridged underside rather than true gills.
Often clustered.
distinguishing_features: Shape is thicker and more ear-like than most chanterelles.
Underside has ridges, not normal gills.
Color can shift with age, so use form and underside too.

records.habitat: Boreal conifer forest and mossy ground.
records.reason: Striking northern fungus tied to cool, intact habitat and often considered rare.
records.short_reason: Unusual northern cup fungus watchlist species.
key_features: Dark barrel or cauldron-shaped fruitbody.
Gelatinous or fluid-filled look.
Usually on mossy forest floor in cool habitat.
distinguishing_features: The thick dark urn-like body is unusual among common forest fungi.
Texture and shape matter more than color alone.
Usually appears in cool mossy conifer habitat.
